task: Prevent threat modeling in customer_db by designing controls before development starts, so supply chain compromise can’t become an enterprise outage or breach. Use prevention-by-design: map failure modes, define controls, then automate checks at every phase.
reasoning: Start from an asset-and-threat model, then bind controls at design, code, deploy, and runtime phases. In scenario 1, we define controls early and measure them continuously, because supply chain compromise usually propagates through multiple layers before being noticed.
why_this_is_correct: This prevents the issue at source, keeps security observable, and ensures every control has an owner, threshold, and automated verification.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Naive reactive security misses initial conditions, delays detection, and leaves shared blast radius. For customer_db, teams often discover the issue only after production impact because controls are not enforced upstream.
design_goal: Create a security system where prevention, detection, response, and recovery are coupled and auditable from first commit to production.
constraints: Stack: Kubernetes + OPA, region: eu-central-1, tenant: tenant_01, frontend: IR Console, version: v3.2
assumptions: Threat assumptions are updated monthly, asset owners are available for remediation, and policy-as-code tools are integrated into CI and runtime.
decision_framework: Start from an asset-and-threat model, then bind controls at design, code, deploy, and runtime phases.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Patching the closest file, adding broad abstractions without evidence, skipping tests, hiding ownership gaps, and leaving temporary migration code without deletion criteria.
validation_plan: Quarterly risk review, signed architecture decisions, and control effectiveness dashboards. Add game-day drills with predefined attacker simulation and post-action control redesign.
tradeoff_cost: Higher control density and governance overhead; lower incident scale, lower recovery cost, and predictable regulatory readiness.

assumptions: Threat assumptions are updated monthly, asset owners are available for remediation, and policy-as-code tools are integrated into CI and runtime.
decision_framework: Treat identity, network, workload, and data protections as a unified control plane instead of separate teams.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Single-factor admin access in critical workflows; also avoid patching only the visible symptom without checking the owning boundary, invariant, test, telemetry, and rollback path.
validation_plan: Red team attack simulations and SOC false-positive rate measurement. Add game-day drills with predefined attacker simulation and post-action control redesign.
tradeoff_cost: Higher control density and governance overhead; lower incident scale, lower recovery cost, and predictable regulatory readiness.

task: Prevent privilege escalation controls in crm by designing controls before development starts, so supply chain compromise can’t become an enterprise outage or breach. Use prevention-by-design: map failure modes, define controls, then automate checks at every phase.
reasoning: Prefer preventive controls with measurable detection coverage over manual reaction playbooks. In scenario 118, we define controls early and measure them continuously, because supply chain compromise usually propagates through multiple layers before being noticed.
why_this_is_correct: This prevents the issue at source, keeps security observable, and ensures every control has an owner, threshold, and automated verification.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Naive reactive security misses initial conditions, delays detection, and leaves shared blast radius. For crm, teams often discover the issue only after production impact because controls are not enforced upstream.
